politician.name: Tammy Sue Baldwin
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative WI-2 (1999-2013); Wisconsin State Assembly (1993-1999)
politician.profile_narrative: Tammy Baldwin represents Wisconsin in the U.S. Senate since January 2013, making history as the first openly LGBT U.S. senator. She won re-election in November 2024 by approximately 1 percentage point against Republican challenger Eric Hovde. She is a member of the Senate HELP and Appropriations Committees. She co-led the Respect for Marriage Act (2022) with Senator Susan Collins, codifying federal recognition of same-sex marriage. Her priorities include healthcare access, manufacturing-sector support, and consumer protection.

promises.promise_text: Co-author and pass Respect for Marriage Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Baldwin co-authored the Respect for Marriage Act with Sen. Susan Collins (R-ME); passed Senate 61-36 on November 29, 2022; signed by President Biden on December 13, 2022. Codifies federal recognition of same-sex and interracial marriages. Approximately 7 million Americans in same-sex marriages affected. Substantive promise outcome achieved.
promises.case_study_narrative: The Respect for Marriage Act was a Baldwin priority across her Senate tenure. After Justice Thomas's Dobbs concurrence (June 2022) questioned the precedential basis for Obergefell v. Hodges, Baldwin and Collins led bipartisan negotiations on a federal codification. The bill repeals the Defense of Marriage Act (1996) and requires federal recognition of any marriage valid in the state where it was performed. It also protects interracial marriage from any future overturning of Loving v. Virginia. Passed 61-36 in the Senate with 12 Republicans joining all Democrats. House voted 258-169-1. Biden signed at a White House ceremony December 13, 2022.

promises.promise_text: Expand Affordable Care Act / public option.
promises.verdict_reasoning: ACA subsidies were extended via IRA (Baldwin YES). Public option did not pass. Medicare drug-pricing negotiation enacted via IRA. Coverage expansion delivered partially through ACA subsidies, not public-option route.

Baldwin's Buy American provisions were incorporated into the Bipartisan Infrastructure Act and CHIPS Act. The CHIPS Act ($280B) drove approximately $400B+ in announced semiconductor manufacturing investments. Build America Buy America (BABA) Act provisions strengthened domestic-sourcing requirements for federal infrastructure projects.

Secured Wisconsin-specific dairy support including DMC (Dairy Margin Coverage) program enhancements in 2018 and 2024 Farm Bill negotiations. Co-sponsored DAIRY PRIDE Act on labeling of plant-based 'milk' products (not enacted but moved labeling discussion).
Voted YES on IRA. Green New Deal resolution did not advance. Substantive climate investment delivered via IRA but at smaller scale than broader framework.
IRA Medicare drug-pricing negotiation passed. Baldwin YES.
PACT Act (August 2022) passed. Baldwin YES.

promises.promise_text: Restore voting rights / John Lewis VRA.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on cloture; failed Republican filibuster + Manchin/Sinema rule-change opposition. Per obstruction rule, external obstruction.
WHPA cloture failed 49-51 (Manchin NO). Outcome not achieved.
PRO Act House-passed; failed Senate filibuster.

promises.promise_text: Bipartisan Safer Communities Act / gun safety.
promises.verdict_reasoning: BSCA passed June 2022 (Baldwin YES). First major federal gun-safety legislation in 30 years.
Voted YES on every Ukraine aid package 2022-2024.
Voted YES on Justice Jackson confirmation (April 2022). Voted to confirm hundreds of Biden lower-court nominees. Voted against multiple Trump-era nominees.
Supported Biden's broad cancellation (SCOTUS-struck-down). Approximately $138B in narrower cancellations approved.

promises.promise_text: Stand up to Big Pharma / lower drug prices.
promises.verdict_reasoning: IRA drug-pricing negotiation passed. Broader Pharma reform (Inflation Reduction Act 2.0 framework, removing patent-extension loopholes) did not pass. Whether the IRA constitutes 'standing up to Big Pharma' depends on framework.

Baldwin campaigned for Harris-Walz in 2024 and won her own Senate race. Trump won the presidency. Whether this counts as a kept or broken promise depends on whether 'defeat Trump' was Baldwin's own goal or a broader Democratic Party goal.

politician.name: Thomas Jonathan Ossoff
politician.current_status: Serving first Senate term (elected January 2021 runoff)
politician.professional_background: Former investigative journalist; CEO of documentary film production company; lost 2017 GA-6 special election to Karen Handel
politician.profile_narrative: Jon Ossoff represents Georgia in the U.S. Senate, elected in the January 5, 2021 runoff that — along with Raphael Warnock's win — gave Democrats Senate control. He is the youngest senator elected since 1973 and the first millennial in the Senate. He chairs the Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations (PSI), where he has led oversight investigations on federal prison conditions, child labor in the U.S., and pharmacy benefit managers. He is up for re-election in 2026.

promises.promise_text: Oversight and investigation of federal agencies.
promises.verdict_reasoning: As PSI chair, Ossoff led major investigations: Federal Bureau of Prisons abuses (2022, led to BOP director resignation); child labor in US food production (2023); pharmacy benefit managers (2024); USPS service degradation (2024). Multiple subpoenas issued; multiple substantive reports produced.
Voted YES on American Rescue Plan ($1.9T, March 2021). Direct payments to constituents totaled approximately $9B in Georgia.
IRA Medicare drug-pricing negotiation passed (Ossoff YES). 10 drugs initially, expanding to 60 by 2029. $35/month insulin cap for Medicare.

promises.promise_text: Restore voting rights / John Lewis VRA.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Cloture votes failed in 2021-2022 (Republican filibuster + Manchin/Sinema rule-change opposition). VRA preclearance not restored. Per obstruction rule, primarily external + 2 same-caucus defectors; outcome not achieved.
IRA passed (Ossoff YES). Green New Deal framework did not advance.
BSCA passed (Ossoff YES). Broader assault-weapons ban did not pass.
PRO Act failed Senate cloture.
WHPA failed cloture (Manchin NO).
Voted YES on all Israel aid packages. Co-sponsored Israel-security legislation.
Voted YES on Justice Jackson + hundreds of lower-court Biden nominees.
Voted YES on IIJA (November 2021).
ARPA expansion expired end of 2021 after Manchin opposition to BBB. Permanent expansion did not pass.
House passed; Senate cloture failed. Respect for Marriage Act passed (Ossoff YES).

PSI investigations covered some Trump-era topics but were primarily focused on bipartisan issues. Senate Jan 6 Committee work was House-led. Whether Ossoff sufficiently 'investigated Trump-era abuses' depends on framework.

politician.name: Joseph Manchin III
politician.current_status: Former senator — did not seek re-election in 2024; switched from Democratic to Independent in May 2024
politician.professional_background: Former Governor of West Virginia (2005-2010); Secretary of State of WV; state legislator
politician.profile_narrative: Joe Manchin represented West Virginia in the U.S. Senate from November 2010 to January 2025. He won three Senate elections (2010 special, 2012, 2018) in a state that has trended sharply Republican federally. As the most conservative Senate Democrat, he held effective veto power over the Biden administration's legislative agenda when Democrats had 50-vote control (2021-2023). He switched party affiliation to Independent in May 2024 and did not seek re-election. His tenure shaped the IRA, killed Build Back Better, and blocked the filibuster carve-out for voting rights.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Manchin voted NO on January 19, 2022 filibuster carve-out for voting-rights legislation (carve-out failed 48-52, with Manchin and Sinema joining all Republicans). He maintained this position consistently. The filibuster remained intact through 2024.

promises.promise_text: Negotiate climate + healthcare legislation through reconciliation.
promises.verdict_reasoning: After killing Build Back Better in December 2021, Manchin re-engaged with Schumer in summer 2022 to negotiate the Inflation Reduction Act. IRA passed August 7, 2022 (Manchin YES). The smaller-but-substantial package was largely his framework: 15% corporate minimum tax, methane fee, $369B clean energy, Medicare drug pricing — all things he agreed to after BBB collapsed.

Coal mining employment in WV declined from approximately 12,000 (2011, start of his Senate tenure) to approximately 8,500 (2024) due to natural-gas and renewable-energy economics. Manchin secured Mountain Valley Pipeline approval in June 2023 (Fiscal Responsibility Act). He extracted coal-favorable provisions in IRA. Industry decline continued for market reasons outside Senate control.

Manchin was a primary architect of Bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (Gang of 21 negotiator). Co-led PACT Act (with Tester). Worked with Republicans on Inflation Reduction Act provisions. Bipartisan voting record was the most cross-party of any Democratic senator during his tenure (Lugar Center Bipartisan Index ranked him in top 3).

Manchin voted FOR cloture on Freedom to Vote Act multiple times but voted AGAINST the filibuster carve-out that would have allowed passage. The substantive outcome — voting-rights restoration — did not occur, with Manchin's filibuster-protection vote being a proximate cause.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on federal spending.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Manchin's opposition killed BBB's $3.5T-$2T spending package — the most substantive fiscal-restraint act of his Senate tenure. He then voted for IRA ($740B in tax/spending but deficit-reducing per CBO). Substantive impact on federal-debt trajectory: limited.
Manchin's opposition to BBB killed universal pre-K and childcare provisions. He did not bring alternate proposals to passage.
Co-sponsored and voted YES on PACT Act (August 2022).
BSCA passed (Manchin YES, June 2022).

promises.promise_text: Oppose Build Back Better's full scope.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Manchin publicly opposed BBB in December 2021 'on a Fox News Sunday appearance' citing fiscal and inflation concerns. The full BBB did not pass.
IRA passed (Manchin YES, August 2022) — his negotiated successor to BBB.
Manchin voted NO on Women's Health Protection Act cloture (May 2022, 49-51 failure). His own vote was the proximate cause of one failed cloture attempt.
Voted YES on Justice Jackson + most lower-court Biden nominees.
IRA included $4B for legacy coal/oil communities. Mountain Valley Pipeline approval secured June 2023. Energy-industry employment continued mixed trajectory by sub-sector.
Voted YES on every Ukraine aid package.
Manchin's voting pattern was consistently center-right within the Democratic caucus. He left the Democratic Party in May 2024 to become Independent, citing partisan polarization.

Manchin's 2018 campaign did not explicitly promise to run in 2024, but supporters expected him to seek another term. He announced in November 2023 he would not seek re-election. He briefly considered an independent presidential run in early 2024 but declined. Whether this is a 'broken promise' depends on whether voters considered his service a multi-cycle commitment.

politician.name: Kyrsten Lea Sinema
politician.current_status: Former senator — did not seek re-election in 2024; switched from Democratic to Independent in December 2022
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative AZ-9 (2013-2019); Arizona state legislator
politician.profile_narrative: Kyrsten Sinema represented Arizona in the U.S. Senate from January 2019 to January 2025. She switched party affiliation from Democratic to Independent in December 2022 (caucusing with Democrats). With Manchin, she held effective veto power over key Biden administration priorities during the 50-50 Senate. She was instrumental in negotiating the Bipartisan Infrastructure Act, CHIPS Act, and Bipartisan Safer Communities Act. She did not seek re-election in 2024; her seat is now held by Ruben Gallego (D).

promises.verdict_reasoning: Sinema voted NO on January 19, 2022 filibuster carve-out for voting-rights legislation (48-52 failure). Maintained position consistently.

Co-led Bipartisan Infrastructure Framework (Gang of 21). Co-led Bipartisan Safer Communities Act negotiations. Co-led CHIPS Act bipartisan coalition. Led 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill negotiations (which collapsed after Republican opposition).

promises.promise_text: Independent voice / not beholden to party.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Switched party affiliation to Independent in December 2022. Voted against key Democratic priorities including filibuster carve-out and broad spending packages. Maintained committee positions through Democratic caucus arrangement.

promises.promise_text: Lower healthcare and prescription drug costs.
promises.verdict_reasoning: IRA Medicare drug-pricing negotiation passed (Sinema YES) — but only after she negotiated for the removal of the 'pill penalty' clawback provision and changes to the negotiation timeline. Substantive drug-pricing reform delivered but at reduced scope from initial framework.
Voted YES on cloture for voting-rights bills but NO on filibuster carve-out that would have enabled passage. Outcome not achieved.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture (May 2022, failed 49-51 with Manchin NO). Did not change filibuster vote to enable passage. Outcome not achieved.
Co-led the bipartisan framework. Voted YES on IIJA.
Co-led BSCA negotiations. Voted YES on BSCA (June 2022).
Voted YES on PACT Act.
Led 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill (Lankford-Murphy-Sinema). Bill collapsed after Trump's public opposition triggered House Republican rejection. Comprehensive reform did not pass.

promises.promise_text: Oppose Build Back Better's full tax provisions.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Sinema opposed BBB's individual tax-rate increases. Forced changes to IRA tax provisions including the corporate minimum tax structure and removal of carried-interest reform.
Co-led CHIPS Act bipartisan negotiations. Voted YES (July 2022).
Voted YES on Justice Jackson. Voted YES on most lower-court Biden nominees but voted NO or 'present' on some progressive nominees.
Voted YES on IRA after negotiating changes. Did not support Green New Deal framework or full BBB climate provisions.
Voted YES on Respect for Marriage Act (November 2022).

politician.name: Lisa Ann Murkowski
politician.professional_background: Former Alaska state legislator; appointed to Senate seat in 2002; won 2010 write-in campaign after losing primary
politician.profile_narrative: Lisa Murkowski has represented Alaska in the U.S. Senate since December 2002. She is the senior Republican woman in the Senate and the second-longest-serving Republican woman ever (after Margaret Chase Smith). She won re-election in 2010 as a write-in candidate after losing the Republican primary to Joe Miller — one of the most extraordinary Senate election outcomes in modern history. She is among the most moderate Senate Republicans, frequently crossing party lines on judicial nominations, healthcare, and women's-issues legislation. She chairs the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee.

promises.promise_text: Defend Alaska's oil, gas, and mining industries.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Murkowski secured ANWR drilling provisions in TCJA (2017). Secured Willow Project approval (March 2023, Biden administration). Multiple Alaska-specific resource provisions in appropriations and authorizations.

Voted NO on 2017 skinny repeal (1 of 3 Republican defectors). Voted GUILTY on Trump's 2nd impeachment (1 of 7 Republicans). Voted YES on BSCA, CHIPS, RFMA. Lugar Center Bipartisan Index top-3 most-bipartisan senator.

promises.promise_text: Defend reproductive rights / oppose Roe overturning.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Murkowski voted NO on Kavanaugh confirmation (October 2018, citing concerns about Roe). She voted FOR cloture on WHPA (May 2022, after Dobbs draft leak). Dobbs (June 2022) overturned Roe with Kavanaugh in majority. Murkowski's vote was consistent with the stated position; the outcome (Roe preservation) did not occur. Per obstruction rule, the proximate cause was the Court's composition + Manchin's WHPA cloture NO — Murkowski's own actions were aligned.

Voted YES on Gorsuch, Barrett (citing qualification standard) but NO on Kavanaugh (citing process concerns). Voted YES on Justice Jackson (April 2022) — one of 3 Republicans. Voted to confirm most Trump and Biden lower-court nominees on qualification grounds.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on federal spending.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on TCJA, CARES, multiple appropriations. National debt rose from $19.9T to $35T+ during her tenure period.
Voted YES on IIJA (November 2021), Gang of 21 negotiator.
Secured Alaska Native–specific provisions in multiple appropriations. Co-sponsored Indian Health Service reauthorizations. Voted YES on Native American legislation.
Voted YES on Respect for Marriage Act (which included religious-liberty protections) — one of 12 Republicans. Supported religious-employer protections.
NATO 2% spending rose from 3 nations (2014) to 23 (2024). Russia invasion was likely larger driver than U.S. pressure.

promises.promise_text: Confirm Trump-era judicial nominees who meet qualification standard.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on Gorsuch, Barrett. Voted NO on Kavanaugh (one of two Republican defectors). Voted YES on majority of lower-court Trump nominees.
Voted NO on 2017 skinny repeal. Voted against Texas v. Azar position.
Voted YES on BSCA (June 2022) — one of 15 Republicans.
Voted YES on Israel aid packages.
Voted GUILTY on second impeachment (February 13, 2021) — one of 7 Republicans. Did not vote to convict on first impeachment.

promises.promise_text: Restore voting rights / pass John Lewis VRA.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Murkowski voted YES on cloture on John Lewis VRA in November 2021 (50-49, failed; Murkowski was the lone Republican YES). She voted against filibuster carve-out. Substantive outcome did not occur.

Voted GUILTY on Trump's 2nd impeachment. Voted YES on BSCA, CHIPS, RFMA. Republican base critics charge that 'oppose extreme partisanship' has become 'capitulate to Democratic priorities.' Framework-dependent.

politician.name: James David Vance
politician.current_status: 50th Vice President of the United States; was U.S. Senator from Ohio (2023-2025)
politician.professional_background: Author of 'Hillbilly Elegy' (2016); former venture capitalist (Mithril Capital, Narya Capital); U.S. Senator Ohio (2023-2025); Trump's 2024 running mate
politician.profile_narrative: JD Vance is the 50th Vice President of the United States, serving since January 20, 2025. He was U.S. Senator from Ohio from January 2023 to January 2025, winning the seat by approximately 6 percentage points over Tim Ryan. He is the author of 'Hillbilly Elegy' (2016), a memoir about his upbringing in Middletown, Ohio that became a bestseller and Netflix film. Vance's political evolution — from 2016 Trump critic ('America's Hitler') to 2024 running mate — is one of the more documented political transformations of the period. His Senate tenure focused on China hawkishness, anti-corporate-power positions on the right, and skepticism of Ukraine aid.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Vance voted NO on H.R. 815 (April 2024 Ukraine aid portion) and earlier Ukraine supplementals. Publicly led Senate Republican opposition to Ukraine aid. Vote-record consistent with promise.

Vance voted against the February 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill, citing insufficient enforcement provisions. Supported Trump-era immigration framework. Border legislation Vance preferred did not pass during his Senate tenure; Trump 2nd-term executive actions begin January 2025 (outside his Senate scoring window).

promises.promise_text: Industrial policy / bring manufacturing back to America.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored Railway Safety Act after East Palestine derailment. Voted YES on CHIPS Act provisions extending to Ohio Intel facility. Promoted anti-globalist trade framework consistent with promise.

Voted against Women's Health Protection Act. Supported state-level abortion restrictions. However, after Ohio's Issue 1 (November 2023) constitutionalized abortion rights with 57% support, Vance's national pro-life advocacy moderated. As VP candidate, supported Trump's 'leave it to the states' framing, which is less restrictive than his initial federal-ban support.

promises.promise_text: Take on Big Tech / break up monopolies.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored bipartisan tech-antitrust legislation. Praised Lina Khan FTC enforcement on certain cases. Voted for TikTok divestment bill.
Voted against Ukraine aid. Supported Israel aid in October 2023-2024 context. Mixed record on Middle East troop deployments.
Co-sponsored expanded Child Tax Credit framework. Vance proposed $5,000 child tax credit in 2024 campaign. Did not advance during Senate tenure.
Voted for TikTok divestment, multiple China-export-control measures. Co-sponsored bipartisan China hawk legislation.
Voted against gun-control measures. NRA A rating.
Voted against major spending packages including Ukraine aid. Supported some Trump-era TCJA extensions. Aggregate debt rose during his Senate tenure.
Voted YES on Mayorkas impeachment articles. Co-sponsored oversight measures.
Supported religious-liberty provisions. Voted NO on Respect for Marriage Act.
Vance praised some IRA drug-pricing provisions while opposing the broader IRA. Mixed record.

promises.promise_text: Oppose woke culture / DEI in federal agencies.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted for amendments defunding federal DEI programs. Substantive policy shift was 2025+ (post-Senate, in VP role).
Vance publicly criticized Trump in 2016 ('America's Hitler,' 'cultural heroin'). Became Trump's 2024 running mate. Whether the trajectory constitutes broken principle or evolved view is contested.

politician.name: Marco Antonio Rubio
politician.current_status: Resigned Senate seat January 21, 2025 to become 72nd U.S. Secretary of State
politician.professional_background: Former Florida House Speaker (2007-2008); 2016 Republican presidential primary candidate; 72nd U.S. Secretary of State (2025+)
politician.profile_narrative: Marco Rubio represented Florida in the U.S. Senate from January 2011 to January 21, 2025, when he resigned to become the 72nd U.S. Secretary of State in the Trump administration. He ran for the Republican presidential nomination in 2016, finishing third. He chaired the Senate Foreign Relations Committee's Western Hemisphere subcommittee and was vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee. His Senate priorities included China hawkishness, Cuba/Venezuela sanctions, and Florida-specific economic and environmental measures.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (2021, signed). Led Hong Kong Human Rights and Democracy Act. Sanctioned multiple CCP officials. Vice chair of Senate Intelligence focusing on China.
Co-sponsored multiple Cuba-sanctions measures. Helped reinstate Cuba on State Sponsors of Terrorism list (January 2021). As Secretary of State, continuing the hawkish framework.
Voted YES on Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett. Dobbs (2022) overturned Roe with all three in majority. Voted NO on Justice Jackson.

Co-sponsored multiple CTC expansion frameworks (with Sen. Mike Lee). TCJA doubled CTC ($1,000 to $2,000). ARPA's expanded refundable CTC (March 2021) lapsed end of 2021 after BBB failed. Rubio's larger CTC framework did not pass as standalone.
Voted YES on all Israel aid packages. Co-sponsored multiple Israel-support measures.
Co-sponsored religious-liberty provisions. Voted NO on Respect for Marriage Act (citing religious-liberty concerns).

Voted NO on most gun-control measures but voted YES on Bipartisan Safer Communities Act (June 2022, 1 of 15 Republicans). Conservative critics noted the BSCA vote as a partial break from absolutist position.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on federal spending.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on TCJA, CARES, BSCA, RESPECT-for-Marriage. National debt rose substantially during tenure.
Voted YES on IIJA (November 2021).
Voted NO on WHPA. Supported confirmation of pro-life justices who overturned Roe.

promises.promise_text: Address Florida hurricane recovery / climate adaptation.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Secured multiple Florida disaster-recovery appropriations after Hurricanes Ian (2022), Idalia (2023), Helene/Milton (2024).
Voted YES on multiple CRA resolutions. Aggregate regulatory state continued to grow.
Opposed JCPOA (2015). Supported Trump 2018 withdrawal. As Secretary of State, continuing Iran-sanctions framework.

Voted YES on H.R. 2 (Secure the Border Act). Voted against February 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill (citing insufficient enforcement). Has consistently voted for border-wall and enforcement appropriations.

promises.promise_text: Comprehensive immigration reform with path to citizenship.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Rubio was a 2013 Gang of 8 architect of comprehensive immigration reform that included path to citizenship. The bill passed Senate 68-32 (Rubio YES) but did not advance in the House. Rubio subsequently abandoned the path-to-citizenship position in 2016 presidential campaign and has not returned to it. Position evolution acknowledged by Rubio publicly.

promises.promise_text: Confirm well-qualified Biden nominees on qualification.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted NO on Justice Jackson. Voted YES or NO on lower-court Biden nominees on case-by-case basis. Whether his standard was applied consistently across administrations is contested.

politician.name: Joshua David Hawley
politician.current_status: Serving first Senate term (up for re-election 2024 — won)
politician.professional_background: Former Missouri Attorney General (2017-2019); former law professor; Yale Law School graduate
politician.profile_narrative: Josh Hawley represents Missouri in the U.S. Senate since January 2019. He won re-election in November 2024 by approximately 14 percentage points. He was first elected at age 38, the youngest U.S. senator at the time. He raised his fist toward January 6, 2021 demonstrators outside the Capitol — an image that became iconic and that he has repeatedly defended. His Senate priorities include anti-Big Tech, anti-China hawkishness, pro-life policy, and labor/anti-monopoly framings unusual for a Republican senator.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored multiple Section 230 reform measures. Co-sponsored TikTok divestment bill. Co-sponsored Tucker Carlson FCC censorship inquiry. Voted YES on Khan FTC confirmation.
Co-sponsored multiple China-restrictive measures including Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act and CHIPS-related export controls.

promises.promise_text: Defend constitutional originalism / confirm conservative judges.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on Barrett (2020). Voted NO on Justice Jackson. Frequent legal critic of progressive jurisprudence.

Voted YES to sustain Pennsylvania electoral objection on January 6-7, 2021. Did not change vote after Capitol breach (one of 6 senators who continued to object after the breach). Voted NO on Electoral Count Reform Act (December 2022). Whether these actions constituted defending election integrity or obstructing certification depends on framework — Hawley raised his fist toward protesters outside the Capitol that morning, image widely circulated.
Voted NO on WHPA. Co-sponsored multiple federal abortion-restriction measures.
Voted NO on BSCA and all federal gun-control measures.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on federal spending.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on TCJA, CARES. Voted NO on IIJA (one of 30 Republicans). Voted NO on IRA. Mixed record.

promises.promise_text: Support working-class families / Manchin-Hawley CTC framework.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored expanded Child Tax Credit framework. Did not advance.
Voted NO on Respect for Marriage Act. Co-sponsored religious-liberty provisions.
Hawley supported Amazon Labor Union (a Republican break from anti-labor traditional GOP position). Voted NO on PRO Act. Mixed/evolving record.
Held multiple oversight hearings on platform moderation. Specific Section 230 reforms did not pass.
Voted NO on Ukraine aid packages 2022-2024.
Voted YES on H.R. 2 framework. Voted against February 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill (insufficient enforcement).
Voted YES on Mayorkas impeachment articles. Investigations occurred via committee work.
Voted YES on Israel aid.

politician.name: Hakeem Sekou Jeffries
politician.current_status: House Minority Leader (2023+); previously House Democratic Caucus Chair
politician.professional_background: Former NY State Assembly (2007-2013); former corporate attorney; succeeded Nancy Pelosi as House Democratic leader in January 2023
politician.profile_narrative: Hakeem Jeffries represents New York's 8th congressional district. He has served as House Minority Leader since January 2023, succeeding Nancy Pelosi. He is the first African American to lead a major U.S. political party caucus in Congress. He chaired the House Democratic Caucus from 2019-2023 and is widely viewed as a potential future Speaker if Democrats regain the House majority. He cast the deciding party-line votes to install (Mike Johnson, indirectly through Democratic non-vote-to-oust) and preserve Republican Speakers in close-margin votes, exercising significant leverage on House governance.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Jeffries has maintained near-unanimous Democratic caucus discipline on key votes 2023-2024. Helped pass IIJA-implementation amendments, defeat motion to vacate Speaker Johnson (May 2024) on bipartisan basis, pass Ukraine/Israel/Taiwan supplemental.
Led House Democratic opposition to multiple Republican investigations including Mayorkas impeachment and Biden impeachment inquiry. Democratic minority oversight limited by minority status.
John Lewis VRA / Freedom to Vote Act passed House 2021 (Jeffries YES) but failed Senate cloture. Outcome did not occur.
Jeffries campaigned for Harris-Walz. Trump won November 2024 election 312-226 in Electoral College. Substantive outcome did not occur.
Voted YES on IRA. Led House Democratic support for climate provisions.
Voted YES on IRA ACA-subsidy extensions and Medicare drug-pricing negotiation.
House passed WHPA (Jeffries YES) multiple times. Senate failed cloture. Outcome did not occur.
Voted YES on IIJA (2021).
No cuts during tenure. Voted YES on multiple defense-of-entitlements amendments.
BSCA passed (Jeffries YES). Broader assault-weapons ban House-passed but failed Senate.
House passed (Jeffries YES). Senate cloture failed. Respect for Marriage Act passed.
Led Democratic House support for Ukraine aid packages. Critical in April 2024 vote helping pass H.R. 815.
Voted YES on Israel aid. Position has been criticized by progressive caucus members for not conditioning aid on humanitarian commitments.

Democratic House votes were instrumental in keeping government funded under Speaker Johnson, including the November 2023 CR, March 2024 minibus, April 2024 foreign aid, September 2024 CR. Cross-party cooperation real; major Democratic priorities did not pass.
Supported Biden's broad cancellation (SCOTUS-struck). ~$138B in narrower cancellations approved by 2024.

politician.name: Elizabeth Lynne Cheney
politician.current_status: Former U.S. Representative WY-At-Large; lost 2022 Republican primary to Harriet Hageman after voting to impeach Trump
politician.professional_background: Former State Department deputy assistant secretary; State Department principal deputy assistant secretary for Near Eastern Affairs (2007-2009); attorney; daughter of former Vice President Dick Cheney
politician.profile_narrative: Liz Cheney represented Wyoming's At-Large congressional district from January 2017 to January 2023. She was House Republican Conference Chair (2019-2021) before being removed from leadership in May 2021 over her opposition to Trump's election-fraud claims. She served as Vice Chair of the House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack on the United States Capitol (2021-2023). She lost her 2022 Republican primary to Harriet Hageman 66-29 after Trump endorsed Hageman and Cheney centered her campaign on her January 6 work.

promises.promise_text: Investigate January 6 attack on the Capitol.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Served as Vice Chair of House Select Committee. Investigation produced 845-page final report (December 2022) with criminal referrals against Trump for inciting insurrection, obstructing official proceeding, conspiracy to defraud the United States, and one count under 18 U.S.C. § 2383. Trump was subsequently indicted (separate DOJ action, August 2023). Cheney's role was central to producing the substantive record.

promises.promise_text: Hold Trump accountable / oppose election denial.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted to certify 2020 electoral results January 6-7, 2021. Voted YES on Trump second impeachment (January 13, 2021, one of 10 House Republicans). Served on J6 Committee. Endorsed Kamala Harris September 2024 in opposition to Trump. Lost 2022 primary as a direct result of these positions.

promises.promise_text: Defend conservative principles within Republican Party.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Cheney consistently voted with Republican party-line on policy issues (tax, abortion, judicial nominations, gun rights, regulation, energy). However, her endorsement of Harris in 2024 was characterized by Republican base as abandoning party. Substantive conservative-policy record consistent; party-loyalty position contested.
J6 Committee work, public testimony, and post-Congressional advocacy consistent with stated commitment. Co-authored bipartisan Electoral Count Reform Act (December 2022).
Supported Trump-era judicial nominations including Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett. House votes on judiciary-related measures consistent with conservative position.
Voted NO on most gun-control measures during House tenure. NRA A rating.
Voted NO on Women's Health Protection Act multiple House passages. Pro-life voting record consistent.
Voted YES on CRA resolutions overturning Obama-era and Biden-era regulations.

promises.promise_text: Strong U.S. foreign policy / oppose isolationism.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted for Ukraine aid (during her House tenure pre-2023). Co-sponsored NATO-strengthening measures.
Endorsed Harris-Walz in September 2024. Trump won November 2024. Substantive outcome did not occur.
Lost 2022 Republican primary to Harriet Hageman 66-29 (August 2022). Trump endorsement of Hageman was the proximate political cause.
Voted YES on TCJA, CARES. Voted NO on multiple Democratic spending packages.
Voted for energy-favorable provisions including Keystone XL framework, oil/gas leasing measures. Wyoming-specific support consistent.

Voted NO on first impeachment (December 2019) on Trump-Ukraine matter. Voted YES on second impeachment (January 2021) after Capitol breach. Whether the position shift was principled (different facts) or inconsistent depends on framework.

politician.name: Adam Bennett Schiff
politician.current_status: Serving first Senate term (elected November 2024); previously U.S. Representative CA-28/CA-30 (2001-2025)
politician.professional_background: Former House Intelligence Committee chair (2019-2023); led House first Trump impeachment inquiry (2019); J6 Committee member; former federal prosecutor
politician.profile_narrative: Adam Schiff is a U.S. Senator from California since January 2025. He previously served in the House from 2001-2025, representing Burbank and surrounding areas. He chaired the House Intelligence Committee from 2019-2023 and led the first Trump impeachment inquiry over the Ukraine matter (2019). He served on the House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack. He won the November 2024 California Senate race over Republican Steve Garvey to fill the seat vacated by Dianne Feinstein's death.

promises.promise_text: Hold Trump accountable / defend democratic institutions.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Led first Trump impeachment inquiry (October 2019); served as House impeachment manager. Served on J6 Committee. Voted YES on both Trump impeachments.
Won November 2024 California Senate race over Republican Steve Garvey by approximately 18 percentage points.

promises.promise_text: Restore voting rights / pass John Lewis VRA.
promises.verdict_reasoning: House passed VRA Advancement Act 2021 (Schiff YES). Senate failed cloture. Schiff's Senate tenure begins January 2025 — no Senate vote yet during this window.
House passed WHPA (Schiff YES). Senate cloture failed.
BSCA passed (Schiff YES). Assault-weapons ban did not pass Senate.
Voted YES on IRA.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid packages during House tenure.
House had no role in judicial confirmations; Schiff's Senate tenure too brief to score substantively.
Voted YES on IIJA.
Voted YES on Israel aid.
Supported Biden's broad cancellation; ~$138B narrower cancellations approved.
ESA-restoration legislation did not pass.
House passed (Schiff YES). Senate failed cloture. RFMA passed (Schiff YES House vote).

politician.name: Gavin Christopher Newsom
politician.current_status: Governor of California (second term; up for term limit January 2027)
politician.professional_background: Former Lieutenant Governor of California (2011-2019); Mayor of San Francisco (2004-2011); business owner
politician.profile_narrative: Gavin Newsom is the 40th Governor of California, serving since January 2019. He was re-elected in November 2022 by approximately 18 percentage points. He survived a recall election in September 2021 by a 62-38 margin. He is term-limited and his second term ends January 2027. He has been widely speculated as a potential future Democratic presidential candidate. His tenure has been defined by California's response to the COVID-19 pandemic, wildfire response, homelessness, and a series of high-profile policy fights with Texas and Florida governors over migration and reproductive rights.

promises.verdict_reasoning: California's homeless population rose from approximately 151,000 (2019) to approximately 181,000 (2023) per HUD point-in-time counts — a 20% increase. Newsom committed approximately $24 billion in homelessness spending across his terms. New programs (CARE Court, Project Homekey, mental-health bond Prop 1) launched. The substantive outcome (significant reduction in homelessness) did not occur. Newsom acknowledged the failure in 2024 public statements.

California expanded Medi-Cal to all income-eligible adults regardless of immigration status (January 2024). State Single-Payer Healthcare bill (AB 1400) did not advance in 2022. Universal coverage approached but not achieved.

Signed Proposition 1 (November 2022, 67-33) constitutionalizing abortion access in California. Signed multiple bills protecting providers serving out-of-state patients post-Dobbs. Made California a 'sanctuary state' for reproductive care.

Signed SB 100 implementation accelerating to 100% clean electricity by 2045. Signed 2022 climate package committing $54B to climate investments. California Air Resources Board adopted 2035 zero-emission-vehicle requirement (most aggressive in U.S.). Emissions targets on track per state inventories.
Signed multiple density and ADU bills. California housing production remained below population growth needs. Median home prices stayed near $850K statewide.
Signed multiple gun-safety measures including stricter ghost-gun laws, age-21 long-gun purchase requirement, microstamping requirements. California gun-violence rates declined modestly during tenure.
Signed multiple immigrant-protection measures. Defended sanctuary-state status. Provided state-funded legal services for immigrants facing deportation.
Increased CalFire budget to historic levels. Pre-positioning of resources improved. Specific wildfire seasons (2020, 2025 LA fires) were severe; mitigation effectiveness contested.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on California spending / balance budget.
promises.verdict_reasoning: California faced an estimated $73B deficit for FY2024 budget. Required spending cuts and reserve drawdowns. Multi-year budget gaps projected through 2027.
Signed universal transitional kindergarten (TK) phase-in, fully implemented by 2025-26 school year. Expanded pre-K access. Education funding hit Prop 98 minimums.

California crime trends mixed: violent crime modestly higher 2020-2023; declined modestly 2024. Prop 36 (November 2024) increased penalties for some drug and theft crimes — Newsom signed implementation legislation despite earlier opposition.
Defeated September 2021 recall 62-38.
Won November 2022 re-election 59-41 over Brian Dahle.

Newsom announced in 2019 that he would not extend the high-speed-rail project beyond the Central Valley segment due to cost overruns. Original LA-to-SF promise abandoned. Central Valley segment under construction.

promises.promise_text: Reform CA criminal justice / reduce incarceration.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Signed multiple criminal-justice reform measures. California prison population declined approximately 25% during his tenure. Prop 36 (2024) reversed some prior reforms.

promises.promise_text: Engage on national stage / consider presidential run.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Newsom declined to run for the 2024 Democratic presidential nomination after Biden's withdrawal, deferring to Harris. He has signaled 2028 interest.

politician.name: Ronald Dion DeSantis
politician.current_status: Governor of Florida (second term; term-limited January 2027); 2024 Republican presidential primary candidate
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative FL-6 (2013-2018); Navy JAG officer (2004-2010); Harvard Law graduate; Yale undergraduate
politician.profile_narrative: Ron DeSantis is the 46th Governor of Florida, serving since January 2019. He was re-elected in November 2022 by approximately 19 percentage points — one of the most lopsided gubernatorial wins in Florida history. He ran for the 2024 Republican presidential nomination, suspending his campaign after the Iowa caucus and endorsing Trump on January 21, 2024. He is term-limited and his second term ends January 2027. His tenure has been defined by Florida's COVID-19 policy approach, anti-DEI legislation, education curriculum reforms, and ongoing legal/political fights with Disney.

promises.promise_text: Reject COVID-19 mandates / 'Free State of Florida.'
promises.verdict_reasoning: DeSantis signed multiple executive orders prohibiting school mask mandates and vaccine passports. Florida reopened schools and businesses faster than most large states. The 'Free State of Florida' framing was his defining 2020-2022 brand. Florida's net population growth during this period suggested some validation of the policy framework; mortality outcomes were similar to comparison states.

promises.promise_text: Restrict 'woke' / DEI in education and state government.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Signed Stop WOKE Act (April 2022). Banned DEI offices at state universities (May 2023). Restricted gender-identity instruction in K-3 (Parental Rights in Education Act, March 2022). Implementation continued despite some federal-court partial injunctions.

Flew migrants from Texas to Massachusetts (Martha's Vineyard, September 2022) — the high-profile political move drew lawsuits but no significant policy impact. Signed E-Verify and immigration-enforcement bills. Did not pass comprehensive state-level reform comparable to Texas Operation Lone Star scale.

Signed 15-week ban (April 2022). Signed 6-week 'heartbeat' ban (April 2023). However, November 2024 Florida Amendment 4 (constitutional amendment establishing abortion right to viability) received 57% support — short of the 60% supermajority required, so the 6-week ban remains in effect. DeSantis campaigned against Amendment 4 but the broad public-support level showed substantial Florida opposition to the 6-week framework.
Signed permitless concealed carry law (April 2023). Florida is 26th state with permitless carry.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on state taxes.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Florida remains a no-state-income-tax state. Signed multiple tax-cut packages. Property-tax-relief measures advanced.

Dissolved Reedy Creek Improvement District (Disney's quasi-governmental zone) — the bill was signed but the dissolution was partially walked back via successor entity (CFTOD). Disney litigation against DeSantis dismissed in federal court. Substantive impact on Disney operations: limited.
Suspended campaign January 21, 2024 after Iowa caucus second-place finish. Endorsed Trump.

Signed Parental Rights in Education Act (March 2022). Signed school-choice expansion (May 2023) making Florida one of the largest universal-school-choice states. Curriculum standards revised including African American history standards (2023, controversial).

Managed responses to Hurricanes Ian (September 2022), Idalia (August 2023), Helene/Milton (September-October 2024). Federal disaster declarations secured promptly. Some critics noted slow recovery in specific affected communities; aggregate response widely praised.
Signed SB 1718 (2023), strongest state-level immigration-enforcement bill, including E-Verify requirement for businesses with 25+ employees and harboring restrictions.
Signed multiple religious-liberty measures. Allowed school chaplains as student-support staff.
Florida has term limits for state legislators and governor. DeSantis has not pushed for federal term limits at the same level as state-level ones.

promises.promise_text: Stand against China / Chinese property purchases.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Signed SB 264 (2023) restricting Chinese-government-affiliated property purchases in Florida. CFIUS-style state review process.
Won November 2022 re-election 59-40 over Charlie Crist — Florida's largest gubernatorial victory margin in 20+ years.

politician.name: Gregory Wayne Abbott
politician.current_status: Governor of Texas (third term; up for re-election 2026)
politician.professional_background: Former Attorney General of Texas (2002-2015); Justice of the Texas Supreme Court (1995-2001); paralyzed below the waist since 1984 tree-fall accident
politician.profile_narrative: Greg Abbott is the 48th Governor of Texas, serving since January 2015. He was re-elected in November 2022 by approximately 11 percentage points. He is the longest-serving Texas governor in modern history. His tenure has been defined by the Operation Lone Star border-enforcement program (March 2021+), abortion restrictions (Texas Senate Bill 8, May 2021; trigger ban post-Dobbs), the February 2021 winter storm Uri response and ERCOT reforms, and high-profile clashes with the federal government over immigration enforcement.

promises.promise_text: Aggressive border enforcement / Operation Lone Star.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Launched Operation Lone Star (March 2021) — state-level border-enforcement program deploying Texas National Guard and DPS personnel. Total state spending approximately $11 billion+ through 2024. Bused approximately 100,000+ migrants to NYC, DC, Chicago, and other Democratic cities. Installed buoy barriers in Rio Grande (2023, federal court partially blocked then allowed). Built razor wire and additional fencing along key crossings.

Signed Texas SB 8 (May 2021) — six-week 'heartbeat' ban with private-enforcement mechanism. Texas trigger ban activated post-Dobbs (August 2022) making most abortions illegal. Documented cases of pregnancy-related medical emergencies under the new framework (Kate Cox case December 2023).
Signed permitless carry (June 2021) — Texas is 21st state with constitutional carry. Signed multiple measures expanding gun-rights.

promises.promise_text: Hold the line on state taxes / no income tax.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Texas remains no-state-income-tax state. Signed multiple property-tax relief packages including $18B 2023 property-tax cut.

promises.promise_text: Energy independence / Texas oil & gas industry.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Defended Texas oil & gas industry against federal regulations. Signed multiple pro-energy-industry measures. Texas oil production hit records 2022-2024.

promises.promise_text: Fix ERCOT / prevent another grid failure.
promises.verdict_reasoning: After February 2021 winter storm Uri (246 deaths, $80-130B in damage, 4.5M Texans without power), Abbott signed ERCOT-reform legislation (SB 3, June 2021) requiring weatherization. ERCOT survived multiple subsequent winter weather events without large-scale outages. Reform substantially adopted but not fully implemented (some weatherization-cost passthroughs deferred).
Signed multiple religious-liberty measures including school-chaplain provisions, Ten Commandments display in classrooms (2024 — court litigation pending).

Pushed multiple school-choice efforts. Universal school-choice voucher proposal failed in Texas Legislature multiple times due to rural Republican opposition. Signed CRT-restriction legislation and curriculum-reform measures.
Banned vaccine mandates for state employees and contractors. Filed lawsuits against federal mandates; most federal mandates were rescinded or blocked by courts.

promises.promise_text: Restrict transgender-related medical care for minors.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Signed SB 14 (June 2023) banning gender-affirming care for minors. Multiple legal challenges pending; Texas Supreme Court upheld in June 2024.

Signed SB 13 (2021) restricting state-fund investment in ESG-aligned firms. Substantive implementation produced limited divestment; some critics argued the policy generated symbolic conflict without significant capital movement.
Signed SB 147 (2023) restricting purchases by foreign-adversary-affiliated entities including Chinese government.
Won November 2022 re-election 55-44 over Beto O'Rourke.

Multiple state-vs-federal conflicts: razor wire installation despite Border Patrol removal orders; buoy barrier defiance of federal court orders; busing migrants to Democratic cities. Texas v. DHS Supreme Court case (June 2024) ruled mostly against Texas on jurisdictional grounds.

Smokehouse Creek Fire (February-March 2024) was largest wildfire in Texas history. State response received mixed reviews — some critics noted slow federal-coordination and ranching-community frustration; defenders cited containment and recovery resources allocated.

politician.name: Gretchen Esther Whitmer
politician.current_status: Governor of Michigan (second term; term-limited January 2027)
politician.professional_background: Former Michigan State Senate Democratic Leader (2011-2015); Michigan State Senate (2006-2015); Michigan House (2001-2006); county prosecutor
politician.profile_narrative: Gretchen Whitmer is the 49th Governor of Michigan, serving since January 2019. She was re-elected in November 2022 by approximately 11 percentage points over Tudor Dixon. She is term-limited and her second term ends January 2027. Her tenure has been defined by Michigan's COVID-19 response (including a 2020 thwarted kidnapping plot targeting her), repeal of right-to-work (2023), restoration of automotive-industry investment, and being a frequent 2024 Democratic vice-presidential and presidential speculation subject (declined both).

promises.verdict_reasoning: Whitmer's defining 2018 campaign slogan. She proposed a 45-cents-per-gallon gas tax increase in 2019; the Republican legislature rejected it. She instead used bonding to fund $3.5B in road repairs (Rebuilding Michigan program). Federal IIJA funds (Whitmer secured) added $7.3B. Michigan road quality improved on PASER ratings but did not reach 'fixed' as Whitmer's framing implied.

Signed repeal of Michigan's right-to-work law (March 2023) — the first U.S. state to repeal right-to-work in nearly 60 years. Restoration of unions' ability to collect dues from non-members in unionized workplaces.
Voters passed Proposal 3 (November 2022, 56-44) constitutionalizing abortion rights. Whitmer signed implementing legislation. Michigan's pre-Roe abortion ban (1931) was repealed (November 2024).

promises.promise_text: Defend Michigan auto industry / EV transition.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Secured approximately $25B+ in announced auto-industry investments including GM Lansing EV facility, Ford BlueOval (initially), Stellantis Detroit assembly. IRA tax credits drove additional investment. UAW strike (Fall 2023) ended favorably for workers with Whitmer support.
Signed Clean Energy Future package (November 2023) requiring 100% clean electricity by 2040. Multiple climate-investment programs launched.

Signed red-flag law, universal background checks, and safe-storage requirements (April 2023) — Michigan's first major gun-safety package in decades. Followed Oxford High School (Nov 2021) and MSU (Feb 2023) shootings.
Signed implementation of Proposal 2 (November 2022, voters approved) constitutionalizing nine-day early voting, automatic registration, and other expansions.
Healthy Michigan Plan (Medicaid expansion) maintained. Expanded Medicaid postpartum coverage.

promises.promise_text: Defend public schools / reverse education-budget cuts.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Increased per-pupil funding to record levels. Universal free school meals program. Adjusted school-aid formula to address funding inequities.
Won November 2022 re-election 55-44 over Tudor Dixon.
Worked with Republican-led legislature 2019-2022; Democratic trifecta 2023-2024. Negotiated tax cuts, infrastructure bonding, and COVID-era responses across party lines in early tenure.
Signed 2019 auto-insurance reform package. Premium reductions occurred but unevenly; some consumer groups argued reforms inadequately addressed PIP changes.
Lead service line replacement program scaled. PFAS contamination response programs launched. Substantive multi-year program; complete elimination of lead service lines pending.

Michigan budget balanced each year. State rainy day fund grew. Whether the spending levels reflect 'reduced' versus 'increased' depends on framework — total state spending grew nominally during her tenure.
